coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of double-layer alginate/carbon dot nanocomposite hydrogel for potential wound dressing application
coredata.dc:creator: Ebrahimi, Somaye
Ehtesabi, Hamide
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a new double-layer sodium alginate (SA) hydrogel was fabricated using green synthesized carbon dots (CDs). The Urtica dioica was used as a natural precursor to synthesize CDs. The casting/solvent evaporation process was employed to fabricate SA-CD double-layer hydrogel. The properties of the SA-CD double-layer hydrogel were examined, including morphology, swelling, water vapor permeability, mechanical and rheological properties. The results show that SA-CD double-layer hydrogels are biocompatible, hemostatic, and flexible. They also have adequate water swelling, a high rate of water vapor transmission, and better mechanical properties, which make them candidates for biomedical applications such as wound dressing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Urtica dioica
Natural precursor
Green synthesize
Biopolymer
Biomaterial
Hydrogels

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) DLS result, (B) TEM image of CDs, (C) XRD pattern of CDs, and FTIR spectrum of (D) U. dioica, (E) CDs, and (F) SA and SA-CDs double-layer hydrogels.

(A) Swelling behavior, (B) Gel fraction, (C) WVTR, (D) Contact angle measurements, (E)Tensile strength, (F) Elongation at break, and (G,H) Gelation time of SA, SA-CD1, SA-CD2, and SA-CD3 double-layer nanocomposite hydrogel films.
SEM images of (A, B) SA, (C, D) SA-CDs, and (E, F) cross-sectional micrographs of SA-CDs. (G) Cell viability test of SA and SA-CDs, (H) Hemostatic tests of control (no treatment) and SA-CDs hydrogel.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of double-layer alginate/carbon dot nanocomposite hydrogel for potential wound dressing application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a new double-layer sodium alginate (SA) hydrogel was fabricated using green synthesized carbon dots (CDs). The Urtica dioica was used as a natural precursor to synthesize CDs. The casting/solvent evaporation process was employed to fabricate SA-CD double-layer hydrogel. The properties of the SA-CD double-layer hydrogel were examined, including morphology, swelling, water vapor permeability, mechanical and rheological properties. The results show that SA-CD double-layer hydrogels are biocompatible, hemostatic, and flexible. They also have adequate water swelling, a high rate of water vapor transmission, and better mechanical properties, which make them candidates for biomedical applications such as wound dressing.

ce:section.ce:para: Wound healing is a complex process. Depending on the severity of the wound dressing, the optimal wound dressing may provide moisture and occlusion, protect against infection and contamination, be easy to apply and remove, and have biocompatibility [1,2]. Moreover, wound dressings play an important role in wound healing, the extraction of excess fluid around the wound, and accelerating the healing process [3]. Alginate hydrogels have shown attractive advantages in the field of wound dressings [4]. Alginate dressingscan create a moist environment around the wound to enhance the healing process and can also be used for easy wound removal and replacement [5,6]. But still, some properties of sodium alginate hydrogel need to be improved and optimized to be able to operate under control.

Carbon dots (CDs) in terms of high biocompatibility, high stability, and excellent water solubility have attracted huge attention and are suitable for functionalization by a variety of polymeric compounds [7–9]. In this study, a new double-layer sodium alginate (SA) hydrogels was fabricated using green synthesized CDs. The Urtica dioica was used as a natural precursor to synthesize CDs via the hydrothermal method. The casting/solvent evaporation process was employed to fabricate SA-CD double-layer hydrogel. Then, the effect of increasing CDs on SA hydrogel was investigated. The properties of the SA-CD double-layer hydrogel were examined, including morphology, swelling, water vapor permeability, mechanical and rheological properties. In addition, the cytotoxicity assay was carried out and the hemostatic ability of the hydrogel was estimated.

First, 1 g of crushed U. dioica was mixed with 20 mL of DI water before being placed in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. 5% (v/v) glycerol was added to a 1.5% (w/v) sodium alginate (SA) solution and allowed to degas for 24 h. 25 mL of solution was poured into the Petri dish to produce the first layer and dried for 24 h at 37 °C. To create the double-layer, Various SA-CD solutions (SA, SA-CD1, SA-CD2, and SA-CD3) containing 0%, 4%, 6%, and 8% CDs were poured onto the first layer and dried for 24 h at 37 °C. The size distribution of CDs was determined using dynamic light scattering (DLS). The morphologies of the samples were observed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM). The crystallinity of CDs was studied using X-ray diffraction (XRD). CDs and U. dioica were analyzed using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). FTIR was done to analyze the chemical composition of fabricated hydrogels.

To measure the swelling capacity, three squares of the double-layer hydrogel were submerged in PBS at 37 °C for different times. The weight of samples was calculated after the excesswater was removed. A direct immersion technique was used to determine the gel fraction of the double-layer hydrogel. To measure water vapor transmission rate (WVTR), the hydrogels were sliced and placed over the top of a water falcon. Falcons were weighed and stored at 37 °C in an incubator, and after 24 h the falcons were weighed again. The contact angles of the hydrogel films were measured and processed with Image J software. Tensile strength and percent elongation at break of the double-layer hydrogels were measured. All dried specimens were sliced and pulled at a 10 mm/min rate with a 1 kN load cell until deformation occurred. The effect of CDs on the gelation process was quantified via dynamic time-sweep rheological tests, by observing the changes in the storage modulus (G') and loss modulus (G″). A scanning electron microscope (SEM) was used to examine the surface morphology and cross-section of the hydrogel films. The viability of L929 cells treated by the SA and SA-CDs (1, 2, and 3) hydrogels was monitored via the MTT test. The hemostatic ability of hydrogel was estimated using an amputated rat tail hemostatic model.

The DLS result (Fig. 1 A) demonstrates that the CDs have an average hydrodynamic diameter of 3.5 nm. Fig. 1B shows that the synthesized CDs are uniform in size and possess a nearly spherical shape. The XRD pattern of CDs (Fig. 1C) has a broad peak at 2θ = 20°, indicating that the CDs are amorphous [10]. The U. dioica FTIR shows a peak at 3282 cm−1, which corresponds to the –OH group. Furthermore, peaks at 2920, 1633, and 1419 cm−1 were associated with C–H, CO, and C–S bands, as shown in Fig. 1D. The FTIR of CDs (Fig. 1E) shows peaks at 2920, 2146, 1583, 1410, and 1096 cm−1 which correspond to theC–H, S–C–N, –CO, C–S, and C–O–C bands. In SA and SA-CDs FTIR spectra (Fig. 1F), the shoulders around 922 cm−1 indicate a C–O–C band attributed to the saccharide structure of the double-layer nanocomposite hydrogel. The peaks around 3277, 1604, and 1414 cm−1 indicate the stretching of O–H and COO−. The decrease in the intensity of these peaks in the nanocomposite indicates the presence of CDs in the hydrogel and its increased hydrophobicity [2,11–13].

The thickness of each layer of the SA-CDs double-layer hydrogels was determined to be 0.45 ± 0.01 mm and 0.55 ± 0.01 mm. Swelling behavior is important for wound dressing applications due to its function in the absorbance of wound fluids. The swelling behavior of the hydrogels was studied (Fig. 2 A). Results show that the integration of CDs can increase water absorption capacity, and double-layer hydrogel films have a high water absorption capacity (124.73% to 394.4%). The values of the gel fraction for SA, SA-CD1, SA-CD2, and SA-CD3 are 25%, 28.57%, 37.63%, and 46.96% which shows gel fraction increases with increasing CDs concentration (Fig. 2B). As the concentration of CDs increases, the surface porosity increases, and as a result, the permeability of water increases. Fig. 2C shows that the WVTR of control, SA, SA-CD1, SA-CD2, and SA-CD3 are 181.528, 124.20, 135.350, 143.84, and 153.88 g/m2 h, respectively.

Wettability is determined based on the chemical composition and surface groups of materials. Because CDs were produced from U. dioica, which includes mostly polyphenols, i.e., aromatic compounds with a –OH group, the density of hydrophobic aromatic groups on the surface of the hydrogels rises when CDs are added to alginate (Fig. 2D), which is a desirable feature for wound dressing. Appropriate mechanical integrity is required for wound dressing candidates to withstand handling by the clinician during use and replacement. The tensile strength (TS) of SA, SA-CD1, SA-CD2, and SA-CD3 double-layer hydrogels was 0.103, 0.131, 0.207, and 0.141 MPa (Fig. 2E), while the elongation at break values were 10.426%, 34.751%, 40.301%, and 26.684% (Fig. 2F), respectively. The strong electrostatic connection between the polymer hydrogel matrix and CDs was responsible for the enhanced mechanical strength of the SA-CDs compared to the SA hydrogel. However, in the case of SA-CD3, the tensile strength and elongation at break decreased because CDs may aggregate in the polymer matrix when the concentration of CDs increases [14]. Time-sweep rheological tests (Fig. 2G and H) illustrate that the addition of SA-CD decreases the elasticity of the hydrogel.

SEM images of SA (Fig. 3 A, B) and SA-CD2 (Fig. 3C, D) hydrogel are shown. The images demonstrated the difference in surface morphology before and after CDs inclusion into the SA hydrogel film. Fig. 3(E, F) shows cross-sectional micrographs of SA and SA-CDs double-layer hydrogel, respectively. The prepared double-layered hydrogels consist of a dense outer layer and a porous sub-layer. The inner layer consists of CDs and establishes an excellent absorption system for wound secretions. The outer membrane (with less WVTR than the inner layer) keeps moisture and prevents the wound drying out. In vitro cytotoxicity was evaluated by the MTT assay for prepared double-layer hydrogels with mouse fibroblast (L929 cells) over a 3-day period (Fig. 3G). The results showed that double-layer hydrogel has excellent biocompatibility. The hemostatic test results (Fig. 3H) demonstrate that the SA-CDs double-layer nanocomposite hydrogel can achieve rapid hemostasis.

In summary, we prepared CDs from green precursors (Urtica dioica) via the hydrothermal method. Then, the effect of increasing CDs on SA hydrogel was investigated. The casting/solvent evaporation process was employed to fabricate SA-CD double-layer hydrogel films. Interaction of CDs with alginate groups and successful incorporation into the hydrogels was demonstrated based on the FTIR spectra. Higher concentrations of CDs showed higher swelling, gel fraction, and WVTR. In addition, the CDs concentration had a direct effect on elongation at break, tensile strength, and hydrophobicity. However, the difference between SA-CD2 and SA-CD3 indicates that increasing the CDs concentration to a certain point improves these characteristics, but this trend may change after that. Cell viability tests on SA-CD hydrogels showed cell compatibility. In addition, the SA-CDs hydrogel has hemostatic behavior. The results showed that SA-CDs double-layer hydrogels can be a candidate for wound dressing.

Somaye Ebrahimi: Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Hamide Ehtesabi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to those who supported them especially vice presidency for research and technology, Shahid Beheshti University.
